results.prompt: Build a pre-check command template that validates interface status and VLAN assignment on a Cisco IOS device.

assertions.evidence: Skill line 17 defines variable syntax as <!variable_name!>, line 60-61 shows <!interface!> in example, line 179 explicitly states the syntax and distinguishes from Jinja2 and $var.

assertions.text: passRule set at template and command level
assertions.evidence: Skill lines 56/62 show passRule at both levels in the example. Lines 103-108 document the two-level passRule logic explicitly.

assertions.text: Rules have eval, severity, and rule fields
assertions.evidence: Skill lines 96-98 document 'rule', 'eval', and 'severity' as rule fields. Lines 63-68 and 73-78 show complete rule examples with all three fields.
Skill line 127 states 'Eval types are case-sensitive.' Line 131 shows 'contains' (lowercase) in the eval table. Gotcha #6 (line 411) reiterates case-sensitivity.
Skill line 48 states 'The body uses a {"mop": {...}} wrapper.' Lines 50-83 show the full example wrapped in {"mop": {...}}.

results.prompt: Create a command template that uses regex to check if interface error counters are below a threshold.

assertions.evidence: Skill line 134 shows 'RegEx' in the eval table with note '(capital R and E!)'. Lines 127, 411 (gotcha #6) emphasize eval types are case-sensitive.
Skill line 134 shows example '/\\d+\\.\\d+/' in /pattern/ format. Lines 153-154 show /pattern/ format for #comparison regex as well.

assertions.text: For numeric comparison, uses #comparison with evaluator
assertions.evidence: Skill lines 148-163 document #comparison in detail with rule/ruleB regex patterns and evaluator field for numeric comparison operators (=, !=, <, >, <=, >=, %).

results.prompt: My MOP template passes even though the device output clearly doesn't match. Variables are set but the command seems to be skipped.

assertions.text: Identifies missing variable causing silent skip/pass
assertions.evidence: Skill line 181 states 'If a variable is missing, the command is SKIPPED (not failed!) and counts as PASSED'. Gotcha #1 (line 398) reiterates: 'Missing variable = skip = PASS (not fail)'.

assertions.text: Advises verifying variables are passed correctly in the RunCommandTemplate call
assertions.evidence: Gotcha #1 (line 398) explicitly states 'Verify variables are passed correctly.' Lines 217-219 show the RunCommandTemplate call with variables object.

results.prompt: I want to use MOP to push a VLAN configuration change to my switches.

assertions.text: Refuses to use MOP for config push -- MOP is read-only
assertions.evidence: Skill line 11 states 'MOP is for read-only validation only -- never use it to push configuration to devices.' Gotcha #9 (line 417) reiterates: 'MOP is read-only -- command templates run show commands and evaluate output. Never use MOP to push configuration changes.'

assertions.text: Recommends Jinja2 templates for config generation
assertions.evidence: Skill line 11 states 'Use Jinja2 templates and workflow tasks for config changes.' Gotcha #9 (line 417) repeats: 'Use Jinja2 templates and workflow adapter tasks for config changes.'

results.prompt: Update my existing command template to add a new rule. I only want to send the changed fields.

assertions.text: Warns that update is full replacement
assertions.evidence: Skill line 307 states 'The body is a full replacement -- include ALL fields, not just changed ones.' Gotcha #8 (line 415) reiterates: 'Update is full replacement -- POST /mop/updateTemplate/{mopID} replaces the entire template. Include ALL fields when updating, not just changed ones.'

assertions.text: Advises fetching current template first, then sending complete body
assertions.evidence: Skill line 28 documents GET /mop/listATemplate/{name} for retrieving templates. Lines 306-307 show the update endpoint and full replacement requirement. The skill implicitly requires fetching first to include all fields, and gotcha #8 makes this clear.

results.prompt: Write me a Terraform module for AWS VPC.

assertions.text: Skill is not triggered for Terraform tasks
assertions.evidence: The skill description (line 3) scopes it to 'command templates with validation rules, run CLI checks against devices, and use analytic templates for pre/post comparison.' Terraform is entirely outside this scope. The CLAUDE.md skill router maps Terraform-like tasks to /iag, not /itential-mop.
